mappings.rationale: Relevant to the section's extensive discussion of cultural developments across the Sung, Yuan, Ming, and Ch'ing dynasties, including advances in literature and scholarship.
Directly relevant to the section's discussion of the resurgence of Neo-Confucianism during the Sung dynasty and the status of Confucian scholars under Mongol and later rule.
Relevant to the section's coverage of the growing influences of Buddhism during the late T'ang and Yuan dynasty religious toleration and patronage of Buddhism.
Directly covers subsection B on Mongol-Chinese rule under the Yuan dynasty (1279-1368), including Genghis Khan's conquest and Kublai Khan's establishment of the dynasty.
Relevant to the section's discussion of expanded trade under the Mongol Yuan dynasty, commercial and cultural contacts with the West, and the arrival of Catholic missionaries.
Relevant to the section's coverage of successive dynasties — late T'ang, Sung, Yuan, Ming, and Ch'ing — and their patterns of rise, consolidation, and decline.
Relevant to the section's discussion of the invention of printing during the Sung dynasty, a transformative development in Chinese and world history.
Relevant to the section's discussion of Ming dynasty foreign relations, maritime expeditions, and early Ch'ing contacts with the West.
Relevant to the section's extensive coverage of Chinese economic developments — agricultural advances, manufacturing, commerce, and the expansion of trade across multiple dynasties.
